instructions: Choose the best answer. After you answer, you'll get an explanation — including why each wrong choice is wrong. Aim to understand the feedback, not just to get a score.

questions.prompt: A state agency issues a regulation on a subject that an existing state statute already addresses — and the two give different answers. Which controls?

options.text: The regulation — it is more recently issued and more specific
The statute — enacted law outranks a regulation on the same subject
Whichever the court finds more reasonable
Both apply; the regulation fills in detail the statute omitted

feedback.correct: Right. Statutes outrank regulations in the hierarchy. An agency issues regulations only under authority a statute delegates to it — so where the two conflict, the statute controls.
feedback.a: Recency and specificity do not determine rank. A regulation is issued under authority granted by a statute; where they conflict, the statute wins regardless of which came later.
feedback.c: This is not a matter of reasonableness. The hierarchy decides: the statute outranks the regulation. The court applies the rule, not chooses between two options.
feedback.d: A regulation that directly conflicts with a statute on the same subject does not supplement it — it loses. Supplementing is different from conflicting.

questions.prompt: Congress enacts a statute that fully occupies a regulatory field. An Arizona statute then tries to regulate the same conduct differently. Before an Arizona court, which applies?

options.text: The Arizona statute — states govern their own residents
The federal statute — the Supremacy Clause makes federal law supreme where Congress has acted
Both, unless they directly and irreconcilably contradict each other
The Arizona statute, because it was enacted by elected state legislators

feedback.correct: Right. The Supremacy Clause places federal law above state law. When Congress occupies a regulatory field, it preempts state law in that field — even where the state law is not a direct word-for-word contradiction.
feedback.a: States do not control the question once Congress has occupied the field. The Supremacy Clause displaces state law that conflicts with — or is preempted by — federal law.
feedback.c: Field preemption is broader than conflict preemption. When Congress occupies a field, state law in that field is displaced even without a head-to-head contradiction between specific provisions.
feedback.d: Democratic enactment at the state level does not override the constitutional hierarchy. Federal law is also democratically enacted and ranks higher under the Supremacy Clause.

questions.prompt: An opposing brief cites a section of the Restatement (Third) of Torts and calls it 'binding authority.' How do you respond?

options.text: It is binding — the American Law Institute has statutory authority to publish Restatements
It is binding only in states that have formally adopted it by statute
It is secondary authority — written by scholars, not enacted or decided by a court, so it cannot bind
It is primary authority because it is an official, published compilation of law

feedback.correct: Right. A Restatement is a secondary source. The ALI drafts it to describe and synthesize the law — it is not enacted by a legislature or decided by a court. A court may be persuaded by it, but it is never required to follow it.
feedback.a: The ALI has no lawmaking power. Restatements carry no force of law. Only a legislature enacting a statute or a court deciding a case creates binding authority.
feedback.b: Even when a legislature uses Restatement language in a statute, the binding source is the statute, not the Restatement. The Restatement itself remains secondary regardless of its influence.
feedback.d: Being published or official does not make a source primary. Primary authority is the law itself — constitutions, statutes, regulations, cases. A Restatement analyzes and synthesizes the law; it is secondary.

questions.prompt: An Arizona trial court (Superior Court) must decide a contract issue. The Arizona Supreme Court decided the exact same issue on point last term. That decision is:

options.text: Binding — the Arizona Supreme Court is a higher court in the same system
Persuasive — the trial court has the right to reach its own conclusion
Binding only if the issue involves a statute rather than common law
Persuasive — courts at any level may disagree with prior decisions

feedback.correct: Right. The Arizona Supreme Court is the highest court in Arizona's system. Its decisions bind all lower courts in that system, including the Arizona Superior Court. That is what 'binding' means — must follow, not may consider.
feedback.b: A trial court does not have discretion to reach a different conclusion when a higher court in the same system has spoken. If the rule needs changing, only the Supreme Court can change it.
feedback.c: Binding effect does not turn on whether the issue involves a statute or common law. A higher court in the same system binds a lower court on any question the higher court has decided.
feedback.d: Lower courts in the same system do not have discretion to disagree with a higher court's precedent. That is the difference between binding and persuasive authority.

questions.prompt: A U.S. District Court sitting in the Ninth Circuit faces a federal statutory question. An on-point Sixth Circuit decision reaches a clear conclusion. That Sixth Circuit ruling is:

options.text: Binding — all federal circuits must follow each other on questions of federal law
Binding — because the Ninth Circuit has not yet decided the question
Persuasive — a sister circuit's decisions are not mandatory authority in another circuit
Irrelevant — district courts may only rely on their own circuit's authority

feedback.correct: Right. The Ninth Circuit binds district courts in its circuit; the Sixth Circuit does not. A sister circuit's ruling is often useful and the court will want to consider it — but the court is not required to follow it.
feedback.a: The circuits are coordinate courts — siblings, not parents of each other. None binds another. Only the Supreme Court of the United States binds all the circuits.
feedback.b: A gap in Ninth Circuit precedent means the district court must decide the question itself; it does not make another circuit binding. Binding effect depends on court hierarchy, not on which court got there first.
feedback.d: District courts regularly look to other circuits for guidance — especially when their own circuit has not addressed a question. The ruling is relevant and worth citing; it is just not binding.

questions.prompt: Under state common law, a landlord owes a duty of reasonable care to guests. The state legislature then enacts a statute addressing the same situation but setting a narrower duty. Which rule governs going forward?

options.text: The common-law rule — it has governed the field longer and courts created it
The statute — where enacted law addresses a subject, it controls over inconsistent common law
Whichever the court finds more consistent with public policy
The common-law rule, unless the statute explicitly states it repeals the common law

feedback.correct: Right. The legislature can displace or modify common-law rules. Where a statute directly addresses the subject and gives a different answer, the statute controls. That is one of the legislature's core functions.
feedback.a: Longevity does not determine rank. The legislature can change common-law rules by statute — and when it does, the statute governs going forward.
feedback.c: Public policy is not the deciding factor once the legislature has spoken. The court applies the statute; it does not substitute its judgment for the legislature's on the question of which rule should govern.
feedback.d: A statute need not explicitly repeal a common-law rule to displace it. When a statute addresses the same subject and gives a different answer, it controls over the inconsistent common-law rule even without express repeal language.

questions.prompt: You are beginning research on an unfamiliar area of federal environmental regulation. Where is the best place to start — and why?

options.text: The Code of Federal Regulations — it contains the binding regulations, so go to the top of the hierarchy first
A treatise on environmental law — secondary sources orient you to the field and point you to the primary authority you need
The most recent Supreme Court opinion — courts have the last word, so start there
A law review article — law reviews cover the cutting edge and are the most current

feedback.correct: Right. Secondary sources are the map; primary authority is the road. A treatise gives you the landscape of an unfamiliar field, explains which regulations matter, and points you to the statutes and cases that govern — so you can read primary authority purposefully rather than blindly.
feedback.a: Opening the CFR without a map is rarely efficient when you do not yet know which agency, which title, or which section addresses your question. A secondary source tells you where to look before you start looking.
feedback.c: The Supreme Court does not address most regulatory questions. Starting there misses most of the field. Secondary sources identify the right level of authority before you go looking for specific primary sources.
feedback.d: Law review articles are valuable but vary widely in coverage and currency. For unfamiliar regulatory terrain, a treatise is usually the better starting point — comprehensive, organized, and regularly updated.

questions.prompt: Before the Arizona Court of Appeals, a party cites a Ninth Circuit decision on a question of Arizona contract law (the case arose under diversity jurisdiction). That Ninth Circuit ruling is:

options.text: Binding — Arizona sits in the Ninth Circuit
Persuasive — federal circuit courts do not bind state courts on questions of state law
Binding — the Ninth Circuit sits above the Arizona Court of Appeals in the federal-state hierarchy
Not relevant — federal courts cannot decide state-law questions

feedback.correct: Right. The Ninth Circuit and the Arizona state courts are two separate systems. The circuit's geographic boundaries determine which federal district courts it binds — not which state courts. On a question of Arizona law, the Arizona Supreme Court is the authoritative court.
feedback.a: Being in the same geographic region does not create a hierarchical relationship between systems. Federal circuit courts bind federal district courts in their circuit; they do not bind state courts in those same states.
feedback.c: There is no federal-state hierarchy of that kind. Federal and state court systems are parallel, not stacked on top of each other. On state-law questions, the state hierarchy governs.
feedback.d: Federal courts routinely apply state law under diversity jurisdiction. The Ninth Circuit's reading of Arizona contract law is substantively relevant and worth citing — it is just not binding on Arizona's own courts.
